questions.a: A controlled entry into the kernel to request a privileged operation.
The unprivileged part of the system where applications run.
The privileged part of the OS that manages hardware and system resources.

questions.q: Name three common file-related system calls.

questions.a: The error code set by the last failing system or library call.

questions.q: What is a trap or exception in this context?
questions.a: A CPU transition into kernel mode caused by a syscall or fault.

questions.q: Why are system calls slower than normal function calls?
questions.a: They cross a privilege boundary and often involve saving state and switching modes.

questions.q: Why does the kernel validate user pointers on syscalls?
questions.a: User memory can be invalid or malicious, so the kernel must prevent faults and corruption.
It safely copies kernel data into user memory with access checks.
It exposes some fast kernel-provided helpers to user space without a full syscall.

questions.q: What is the role of an ABI in syscalls?
questions.a: It defines how arguments, registers, and return values are passed to the kernel interface.

questions.q: Why do many syscalls return -1 on error and set errno?
questions.a: It gives a consistent failure signal while preserving a detailed error code.

questions.q: When is batching syscalls worth it?
questions.a: When you can reduce boundary crossings enough to offset the extra complexity.

questions.q: Why would you prefer a library call over a syscall when possible?
questions.a: Library calls stay in user space and are usually much cheaper.

questions.q: What tradeoff does a restrictive seccomp policy introduce?
questions.a: It reduces attack surface, but it can break legitimate functionality if the filter is too narrow.

questions.q: Why can async interfaces still rely on syscalls under the hood?
questions.a: The runtime may batch or poll with syscalls, but the application sees a higher-level abstraction.

questions.q: When is a raw syscall wrapper useful?
questions.a: When you need exact kernel behavior, new features, or a minimal dependency path.

questions.q: What tradeoff do kernel interfaces make compared with direct hardware access?
questions.a: They add overhead, but they provide safety, fairness, and portability.
